item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked it, a conscious use of cliches can result in really cool things. I only noticed that there could be a bit more to Dean after going on the discussion page, but the first impression this one has left on me was a very positive one. I do have a soft spot for overpowered spooky scary murder monsters with a human side though :')

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
at some points i was facepalming at the 'he can do whatever he wants' stuff, and it had that 2008-2010 era scp wiki x-men mary sue stank.

It's the Cliche contest, I don't know how you could write something with those cliches and not have it come off that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hol up.
did you just imply that SCP-2006 is Phobos, Greek God of fear? that uh, fits interestingly into my headcanon. i think its pretty cool how because they're siblings they can both do the shapeshifting thing, makes me wonder if 4606's true form is a sphere like 2006.

at some points i was facepalming at the 'he can do whatever he wants' stuff, and it had that 2008-2010 era scp wiki x-men mary sue stank. i was kinda thinking that at the end he would take advantage of the foundation's hospitality then go back on the deal and attack them in a sorta 'don't deal with the devil' way. still thought it was alright, if maybe a bit shallow. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like this. I mean, it's Deimos! One of the most underused Greek Gods. But the execution was lacking everywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the juxtaposition of the clinical tone in the SCP, description and addendums compared to the logs. SCP 4606 has a lot personality that shines through.

I think the Thaumiel tag is accurate. I found this specific SCP looking through the comprehensive list of Thaumiel SCPs in hopes of finding an SCP to link to for something I am writing. This SCP is on my short list of SCPs I'm interested in referencing.

+1 great concept, spurred my own creative juices, complex enough structure to be interesting but not so much as to bog down your concept

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviews are dated several years after all but one of the dispatches listed in the table. Error?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thaumiel SCPs are used to aid or help in the containment of other dangerous SCPs. Omega-7 is a different case, and Able is too unpredictable and dangerous to even be classified as a Thaumiel.

Oh and just because there's a mention of a pocket dimension in the article doesn't mean that the SCP is somehow related to 106. That's like making the case that 689 is related to 173 simply because they both kill people if you look away from them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think the Thaumiel designation makes sense. After all, Thaumiel Scips are specifically putting keters in check. Just being an asset to the foundation doesn't make a Thaumiel, as evidenced with Pandoras Box/Omega Seven and Able.

There also seems to be a connection with 106 (World War II setting, pocket dimension, tar like substance, enjoys causing fear) that I wish was explored more. The rest of the article was really good for the cliches it explored and I love the O5 dialog. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again I'm fairly dumb when it comes to… remembering anything that isn't in my immediate purview. Still, if everyone else got it, and if dumbdumbs like me still enjoyed the log, I'd say its an accomplishment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually thought it was still too obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed with GentleGifts.

And I was trying to figure that out for like the longest time now until he pointed it out, outstanding work on concealing that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More or less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's 2006, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had a similar kind of reaction; it's not too bad, and given the cliche subject matter having this not devolve into a horrendous trainwreck of an article is commendable in itself, but it falls short of the ambition - having the Greek god of terror as an SCP sets up expectations for some wow moment that never materialised.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not perfect. It tries perhaps a few too many things, and cliches are delicate things to deal with.

But, it's ambitious and bold and I deeply enjoyed it.

Also, did you just suggest that Bobble the Clown is actually Phobos, son of Aphrodite and Are, God of Panic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a comment planned out until the internet crashed.
blehh...

What I'll specifically point to is the issue Floppy points out is that there's not enough here to convince me the O5s would actually put this guy into service based off one O5's account and the scroll contract Deimos devised. Though I absolutely love Nine's reaction and outbursts.

The characterizations are better than him actually containing outside SCPs in the table I'd say. If and when you rewrite it, given how much space you're really allocated for a single SCP article, I'd focus on multiple iterations of different O5s meeting him through multiple wars.

This does a couple of things:

  • You can reasonably go as far back as WW1 or hell even as early as the Napoleonic War if you want to expand on Deimos via journals or even first-hand accounts (if we're going off the premise that O5s are immortal, eldritch beings).
  • Gives variety and subtext to the entity. You see him grow throughout these eras. Perform amazing feats sure, but always show some character growth interspersed.
  • Much like 4450 which was namedropped, you can expand on different journal entry formats, character interactions, and POVs.

Because the character itself is strong. I think that part above all is worth salvaging upon rewrite. Now, I think you tried to do it through the table format, but in reality, I don't think that was the strongest performance of who he was. And I think its a case of readers instinctively drawn to the fact your using really big names which draws the eye away from the main entity's pathos itself.

If you want to keep the table list, I'd recommend applying that with very simple, Series I creatures with more compact descriptions. Ones where you can focus on the entity's main avenue of inflicting terror after two parties are already in conflict or "in the midst of battle."

Elsewise, you could probably get away with having the O5's decision to even allow it near big-bad-keters and world enders like 2669 as a "PENDING" option, refocusing purely on the logs and Deimos' growth throughout eras.

Or even a single conflict. This entire thing could've been scoped to WW2 honestly with multiple O5s.

These are all just suggestions I had to problems I saw in the final piece. I wish I could've contacted you earlier but my hands were really tied this week.

As it stands it's a novote atm though will change to upvote if the piece receives revisions. As it stands, I loved the logs man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Response
- hide
  • to answer your first question, I don't think it's relying too much on crosslinks to other articles to tell it's story. I made this purely in mind as a Thaumiel that can contain Sapient Keters, and to do that without trying to kill them. Using fear as a way of keeping them in place was the perfect route. You are correct in your assumptions that this is nothing more than an employee for the Foundation. He literally said he wanted a job that would allow him to use his powers as a god, and being in the Foundation would do so. He's only classified as an SCP because the Council wants to and considering how many names and identities he's tsken over the centuries, I don't think he would mind being called anything else.
  • True. They don't really trust it, as it's only on the approval of O5-3 and their contract, but they really can't pass up an opportunity for an answer that takes care of their Keters that break out of containment once a week. Which also brings me to my next answer:
  • Firstly, the portals exist as a gateway of sorts into its dimension. This is answered in the addenda which clearly states that it is only allowed access to the site it is kept in. Gods cannot break their own laws and rules once they're set in stone. I'm sure that the Foundation is aware of this fact and uses it the way one would have a contract with a company.

All in all, I do plan on revisiting this after the contest is over. I also plan on making revisions to this since the only reason why I posted it was because I was almost out of time to post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems sort of prototypical for the SCP it's aiming for. Like, the entity has the laid back personality his age suggests, and he has one war buddies with an O5. Like: "There, he's justified." The style was addequate in itself, but there were no "wow" moments for such a force o' nature. If anything the description of what he does are the most difficult parts to parse. I could see some additional or different character writing really make the guy interesting, rather than just super duper powerful dude. No vote here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, I do. The conceit of an ancient deity classified as a modern-day anomalous entity is interesting to me. But I have numerous gripes with the execution that rob me of how much I would enjoy this. I think its reliance on other articles to tell its story is too prominent: I came here to read about and understand this anomaly, not to gain a better understanding of the established properties of other articles by proxy. There are also aspects of the story here that strain my suspension of disbelief, and I don't think there is quite enough here to separate itself from other mythological demigods-turned-SCPs out there.

feedback/rant
– hide block

I'll start off by explaining what I do like. I like the portrayal of the O5's employment of the reality bender. It makes sense in the contest of the article and the Foundation in general: reality benders are far too important to piss off, so having a placated reality bender on your staff is an exceptional boon.

After mulling this over, though, I actually think it's the execution of cliches here that doesn't work:

  • The article doesn't make me feel that this entity is a spooky murder monster. It tells me that it is, and compares it to other articles in an attempt to display that, without going into the properties that make it so. When an SCP relies so heavily on other articles as a means of comparison, it does neither its own article nor any crosslinked articles any justice. It's essentially saying "my entity is more powerful, look at what it can do to these powerful SCPs!". And that doesn't appeal to me.
  • While it makes sense that the O5s would want to employ a powerful being, how they came to trust it doesn't. This actually ties in with my next gripe as well:
  • The portal it can create. Why is the portal necessary to tell this story? If the entity can portal out of the site at any time, then why does it bother staying contained? I can't think of any reason why it would continue to be contained, other than because it's a Foundation employee that happens to know O5-3.

These aren't issues unique to this article. I've seen issues plaguing cliche justification throughout the contest. The point of the contest is to recontextualize old cliches in a manner that gives them new life. They're cliches for a reason: they're overused concepts that do little to make a story original. You can use a cliche as a means of telling a better story, but you have to do extra story work to make your use of the concept unique and interesting in its own way. And while I don't think you were able to execute your concept that well here, I would love to see a version of this written after the contest that doesn't rely on cliches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damned fine! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As benders go, this is a great take. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pure genius.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing in life is to be feared. It is to be understood.

- Marie Curie

The clichés I've chosen for this are:

  • Spooky scary murder monster.
  • An anomaly so helpful/harmless, the Foundation lets it roam the Site and has minimal containment procedures.
  • A doorway of any shape to a different dimension/timeline/world (not sure if this counts but oh well).

This is probably the most ambitious article I've written so far, hopefully it sticks well.

Thanks to KindlyTurtleClem for forming most of the critique.

Image attribution:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:%22As_you_were,%22_(1919)_(14782105695).jpg
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:It_starts_with_a_crack..._(35815932072).jpg
